question_text: A 58-year-old man presents with gross hematuria. CT urogram shows a 3.5 cm heterogeneously enhancing solid mass arising from the upper pole of the right kidney with enhancement of 85 HU on the nephrographic phase. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is the most common primary renal malignancy in adults (85% of cases). Enhancement >15–20 HU on CT is the key indicator of vascularity/malignancy. Clear cell RCC typically shows marked heterogeneous enhancement. Angiomyolipoma contains macroscopic fat (negative HU values). Oncocytoma may appear identical to RCC on imaging and is a benign diagnosis of exclusion — it cannot be reliably distinguished from RCC by CT alone. Renal abscess presents with fever, positive urine culture, and rim-enhancing fluid collection.
reference: Silverman SG et al. AJR 2019;213:837-845

question_text: On CT, an adrenal mass measures 8 HU on unenhanced images, 68 HU on portal venous phase, and 22 HU on 15-minute delayed images. Which calculation is most useful in characterizing this as a lipid-poor adenoma?
option_a: Absolute percentage washout (APW) = [(Enhanced HU − Delayed HU) / (Enhanced HU − Unenhanced HU)] × 100
option_b: Relative percentage washout (RPW) = [(Enhanced HU − Delayed HU) / Enhanced HU] × 100
option_c: Net enhancement = Enhanced HU − Unenhanced HU
option_d: Signal intensity index on chemical shift MRI
explanation: For a lipid-poor adenoma (unenhanced HU >10), absolute percentage washout (APW) ≥ 60% is diagnostic of adenoma. In this case: APW = [(68 − 22) / (68 − 8)] × 100 = [46/60] × 100 = 77% → adenoma. RPW ≥ 40% can also be used when unenhanced images are unavailable. In this question the unenhanced HU is 8 (lipid-rich adenoma), but the APW formula remains the most specific criterion for characterizing lipid-poor adenomas when all three phases are available. Chemical shift MRI uses the signal intensity index and is the MRI equivalent approach.
reference: Blake MA et al. Radiology 2003;228:695-700

question_text: A 45-year-old man presents with acute scrotal pain of 4 hours duration. Scrotal ultrasound shows absent intratesticular blood flow on Doppler and a 'whirlpool sign' of the spermatic cord. What is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: MRI of the scrotum for further characterization
option_c: Urology consult in 24 hours; repeat Doppler in 6 hours
explanation: This is testicular torsion — a surgical emergency. The absent Doppler flow and 'whirlpool sign' (twisting of the spermatic cord) are diagnostic. Salvage rates are >90% within 6 hours, dropping to <50% at 12 hours, and near zero at 24 hours. Immediate surgical exploration with bilateral orchiopexy is the standard of care. MRI and further imaging only delay treatment. Antibiotics would be appropriate for epididymo-orchitis, which presents with increased (not absent) Doppler flow.
reference: Dogra VS et al. Radiology 2003;227:18-33

question_text: On multiparametric MRI of the prostate, a 62-year-old man has a 1.5 cm lesion in the peripheral zone showing markedly low T2 signal, markedly restricted diffusion (ADC 580 mm²/s), and early arterial enhancement on DCE. According to PI-RADS v2.1, what category is this lesion?
explanation: In the peripheral zone, DWI is the dominant sequence for PI-RADS v2.1 scoring. A lesion ≥15 mm with markedly restricted diffusion (markedly low ADC + markedly high signal on high b-value DWI) scores DWI 5. Any peripheral zone DWI score of 5 = overall PI-RADS 5, regardless of T2 or DCE. This 1.5 cm lesion with ADC ~580 mm²/s and markedly restricted diffusion meets the DWI 5 criterion (size ≥15 mm is not required when diffusion is markedly restricted). DCE positivity adds further support but does not change the category. PI-RADS 5 carries >88% PPV for clinically significant cancer.
reference: PI-RADS Steering Committee. Radiology 2019;292:278-296

question_text: A 72-year-old woman is found to have a right adrenal mass incidentally. On CT, the mass measures 2.5 cm and contains areas of macroscopic fat (−30 HU). What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Macroscopic fat within an adrenal mass is virtually pathognomonic of myelolipoma. Myelolipomas contain hematopoietic marrow elements and mature fat, resulting in areas of HU values ranging from −30 to −100. They are benign, non-functioning tumors requiring no treatment unless large (>4–5 cm) due to risk of hemorrhage. Adrenal metastases show no macroscopic fat. Pheochromocytoma can occasionally contain microscopic lipid but is predominantly soft tissue. ACC is typically large (>6 cm), heterogeneous, and aggressive.

question_text: A 35-year-old woman with tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) has multiple bilateral renal lesions containing macroscopic fat. The largest measures 5.5 cm. She presents with acute flank pain and CT shows a perirenal hematoma. What is the most likely diagnosis and complication?
option_a: Renal cell carcinoma with spontaneous rupture
option_b: Angiomyolipoma with Wunderlich syndrome (spontaneous retroperitoneal hemorrhage)
explanation: Angiomyolipoma (AML) is strongly associated with TSC (approximately 80% of TSC patients have renal AML). The hallmark is macroscopic fat on CT (HU < −10). Lesions >4 cm carry increased risk of spontaneous hemorrhage (Wunderlich syndrome — spontaneous non-traumatic retroperitoneal hemorrhage), attributed to aneurysm formation within abnormal intratumoral vessels. Prophylactic embolization or ablation is recommended for AMLs >3–4 cm or those growing rapidly. Fat-poor (lipid-poor) AMLs can mimic RCC on CT.
reference: Flum AS et al. J Urol 2016;196:1345-1352

question_text: A 55-year-old man undergoes CT for flank pain. An incidental renal cyst is seen with ≤3 hairline-thin septa and no measurable enhancement on multiphasic CT. It measures 4 cm. According to the 2019 Bosniak classification, what category is this cyst and what is the recommended management?
option_a: Bosniak I — simple cyst, no follow-up
option_b: Bosniak II — minimally complex, no follow-up required
option_d: Bosniak III — surgical resection or ablation recommended
explanation: Under the 2019 Bosniak classification (Silverman et al.), a cyst with ≤3 hairline-thin septa and no measurable enhancement is classified as Bosniak II, regardless of size. Bosniak II cysts have a <5% malignancy risk and require no follow-up. The IIF category requires ≥4 hairline-thin septa, any smooth thickening, or applies to uniformly high-attenuation (homogeneously hyperdense, ≥70 HU) cysts >3 cm on unenhanced CT. Size alone does not upgrade a non-enhancing cyst with ≤3 thin septa from Bosniak II to IIF. Bosniak I = anechoic/simple cyst with no septa. Bosniak III and IV require surgical evaluation.
reference: Silverman SG et al. Radiology 2019;292:484-497

question_text: A 65-year-old man with a history of diabetes presents with fever and right flank pain. CT shows streaky perirenal fat stranding, parenchymal gas, and loss of normal renal architecture on the right. Blood cultures are positive for E. coli. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Emphysematous pyelonephritis (EPN) is a life-threatening necrotizing infection with gas production within the renal parenchyma, collecting system, or perinephric space. It is almost exclusively seen in diabetics (90% of cases). CT is the gold standard — demonstrating intraparenchymal gas, parenchymal destruction, and perirenal involvement. The Huang and Tseng classification guides management: Class 1–2 (gas in collecting system or parenchyma only) → antibiotics + drainage; Class 3–4 (extensive gas or bilateral) → nephrectomy often required. Mortality is 20–40% despite treatment.
reference: Huang JJ, Tseng CC. Arch Intern Med 2000;160:797-805

question_text: A 28-year-old man undergoes fluoroscopic retrograde urethrogram after a pelvic fracture. Contrast extravasates superior to the urogenital diaphragm into the periprostatic and perivesical space. The bladder neck appears intact. What type of urethral injury does this represent?
option_b: Type I posterior urethral injury — stretching without tear
option_c: Type II posterior urethral injury — tear at prostatomembranous junction above urogenital diaphragm
option_d: Type III posterior urethral injury — tear extending through urogenital diaphragm into bulbar urethra
explanation: The posterior urethra is most commonly injured in pelvic fractures. The Goldman classification describes three types. Type I: stretching/elongation without tear — contrast fills the urethra normally. Type II: tear at the prostatomembranous junction — contrast extravasates above the urogenital diaphragm into the periprostatic/perivesical space. Type III: complete disruption through the urogenital diaphragm — contrast extravasates both above and below it into the perineum. This case shows contrast above the diaphragm only, with intact bladder neck = Type II. The retrograde urethrogram (RUG) is the first-line imaging study for suspected urethral injury.
reference: Goldman SM et al. J Urol 1997;157:85-89

question_text: A 40-year-old woman presents with cyclical pelvic pain and infertility. Pelvic MRI shows bilateral adnexal cysts with T1 high signal that does NOT suppress on fat saturation, and T2 low signal 'shading.' What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Endometriomas (chocolate cysts) are the classic T1 bright, T2 dark ('T2 shading') lesion on MRI. The key distinction from dermoid: T1 high signal that does NOT suppress on fat saturation, because the signal arises from blood products (hemosiderin, methemoglobin), not fat. Dermoid cysts show T1 high signal that DOES suppress on fat saturation. T2 shading results from recurrent hemorrhage with concentration of blood breakdown products. Bilateral adnexal endometriomas in a woman with cyclical pain and infertility is the classic presentation.
reference: Togashi K et al. Radiology 1991;180:729-733

question_text: On ultrasound, a 25-year-old male has a hypoechoic intratesticular mass. Tumor markers show elevated AFP and beta-hCG. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Non-seminomatous germ cell tumors (NSGCTs) — including embryonal carcinoma, yolk sac tumor, choriocarcinoma, and mixed GCT — are associated with elevated AFP and/or beta-hCG. Pure seminomas may have mildly elevated beta-hCG (via syncytiotrophoblastic cells) but do NOT produce AFP — elevated AFP always indicates a non-seminomatous component. NSGCTs tend to be more heterogeneous on ultrasound, with cystic, hemorrhagic, and calcific components. Leydig cell tumors produce androgens/estrogens. Epidermoid cysts are benign with a characteristic 'onion-skin' layered echotexture.
reference: Woodward PJ et al. Radiographics 2002;22:189-216

question_text: A 30-year-old woman in the 8th week of pregnancy presents with acute pelvic pain and vaginal bleeding. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus and a 2 cm tubal ring sign in the right adnexa with free pelvic fluid. Serum beta-hCG is 2,500 mIU/mL. What is the next most appropriate management step?
option_a: Repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours; repeat ultrasound if rising
option_b: MRI of the pelvis for characterization
option_c: Immediate gynecology referral for ectopic pregnancy management
explanation: The 'tubal ring sign' — an echogenic ring surrounding a gestational sac-like structure in the adnexa — combined with an empty uterus and free pelvic fluid is diagnostic of tubal ectopic pregnancy. The discriminatory zone for beta-hCG is 1,500–2,000 mIU/mL — at this level, an intrauterine pregnancy should be visible on transvaginal US. An empty uterus above the discriminatory zone with adnexal findings and free fluid indicates likely ruptured ectopic — requiring urgent gynecologic evaluation. Methotrexate is used for stable unruptured ectopic; surgical management if hemodynamically unstable.
reference: Barnhart KT. NEJM 2009;361:379-387

question_text: A 55-year-old man undergoes CT cystogram after motor vehicle accident. Contrast injected retrograde through a Foley catheter extravasates into the paracolic gutters and between bowel loops. No contrast is seen in the perivesical fat. What type of bladder injury is this?
option_d: Combined intra- and extraperitoneal bladder rupture
explanation: Intraperitoneal bladder rupture occurs when blunt trauma ruptures the dome of the bladder (the only peritoneum-covered portion), allowing urine to freely enter the peritoneal cavity. CT cystogram findings: contrast in the paracolic gutters, between bowel loops, and around the liver/spleen. Treatment is surgical repair. Extraperitoneal rupture (most common, associated with pelvic fractures) shows contrast limited to the perivesical fat and pelvis — 'starburst' or 'molar tooth' pattern. IMPORTANT: always retrograde-fill the bladder with 350 mL contrast for adequate CT cystogram — an inadequately distended bladder can give false negatives.
reference: Vaccaro JP et al. AJR 2000;174:89-95

question_text: MRI of the pelvis for cervical carcinoma staging shows a 4.2 cm cervical mass with disruption of the low T2 signal cervical stroma and extension into the parametrium, but the pelvic sidewall is not involved and there is no hydronephrosis. What is the FIGO stage?
explanation: FIGO 2018 staging: Stage I = confined to cervix. Stage IIA = upper 2/3 vaginal extension, no parametrial involvement. Stage IIB = parametrial involvement WITHOUT pelvic sidewall involvement. Stage IIIB = pelvic sidewall involvement OR hydronephrosis/non-functioning kidney. Stage IVA = bladder/rectal invasion. This case shows parametrial involvement with no sidewall disease or hydronephrosis = FIGO IIB. MRI is the modality of choice for local staging — disruption of the low T2 cervical stromal ring is the key MRI sign of parametrial invasion.
reference: Bhatla N et al. Int J Gynaecol Obstet 2018;143:129-135

question_text: A 68-year-old man with a history of smoking has a CT KUB showing a 1.5 cm filling defect in the mid-ureter with proximal hydronephrosis. Ureteroscopy confirms urothelial carcinoma. Which surveillance protocol is most appropriate after nephroureterectomy?
option_a: No follow-up needed if resection margins are negative
option_c: Cystoscopy every 3 months for 2 years, then every 6 months; plus CT urogram annually
option_d: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast at 6 months only
explanation: Upper tract urothelial carcinoma (UTUC) has a high risk of synchronous (10–20%) and metachronous (30–50%) bladder urothelial carcinoma. Post-treatment surveillance includes cystoscopy every 3 months for 2 years (then every 6 months) and urine cytology due to this intravesical recurrence risk. CT urogram annually assesses the contralateral upper tract. Risk factors for recurrence include Lynch syndrome, high-grade tumors, multifocal disease, and positive cytology. EAU guidelines recommend surveillance based on risk stratification.
reference: Rouprêt M et al. Eur Urol 2021;79:62-79

question_text: A 50-year-old woman undergoes pelvic MRI for abnormal uterine bleeding. The uterus appears globular with diffuse thickening of the junctional zone measuring up to 18 mm and multiple small T2 hypointense foci within the myometrium. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Adenomyosis is characterized on MRI by: (1) junctional zone (JZ) thickening ≥12 mm (normal ≤8 mm; 8–12 mm equivocal), (2) globular uterine configuration, and (3) T2 hypointense myometrial foci or small T1/T2 bright foci (ectopic endometrial islands and hemorrhage). The JZ threshold of ≥12 mm is the most reproducible diagnostic criterion. Fibroids are discrete masses with well-defined borders and a pseudocapsule, causing focal mass effect rather than diffuse infiltration. Endometrial carcinoma involves the endometrial cavity with disruption of the JZ but does not cause diffuse JZ thickening.
reference: Reinhold C et al. Radiographics 1999;19:S147-60

question_text: On non-contrast CT, a left adrenal mass measures 35 HU and is 4.5 cm. On enhanced CT, it measures 110 HU at 60 seconds and 75 HU at 15 minutes. The APW is 32% and RPW is 32%. What is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: No further workup; this is a benign adenoma
option_b: Adrenal protocol MRI with chemical shift sequences
explanation: APW <60% and RPW <40% with unenhanced HU >10 means the mass cannot be characterized as a lipid-poor adenoma by CT washout alone. The next step is chemical shift MRI — in-phase and opposed-phase sequences. Lipid-rich adenomas show a drop in signal intensity (SI) on opposed-phase images (SI index >16.5% drop). If MRI is also indeterminate, FDG-PET can help: adenomas are typically FDG-negative, malignant masses are FDG-avid. CT-guided biopsy is reserved for known primary malignancy where confirming adrenal metastasis changes management — and only after ruling out pheochromocytoma biochemically.
reference: Boland GW et al. AJR 2008;190:1623-1633

question_text: A 42-year-old man with hypertension and episodic headaches, palpitations, and diaphoresis undergoes CT. An adrenal mass is found measuring 5 cm with heterogeneous enhancement and central necrosis. 24-hour urine metanephrines are elevated. What is the most important management principle before any procedure?
option_a: Proceed immediately with CT-guided biopsy to confirm the diagnosis
option_b: Start beta-blockade immediately without alpha-blockade to control heart rate
option_c: Rule out pheochromocytoma biochemically before any invasive procedure; alpha-block first if confirmed
option_d: Administer IV contrast for adrenal protocol CT washout study
explanation: Pheochromocytoma is the classic 'rule of 10s' tumor: 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal (paraganglioma), 10% malignant, 10% in children. CT shows a heterogeneous, avidly enhancing mass with central necrosis; MRI shows marked T2 hyperintensity ('light bulb' sign). CRITICAL principle: NEVER biopsy or perform surgery on an adrenal mass without first ruling out pheochromocytoma biochemically (24-hour urine metanephrines/catecholamines or plasma metanephrines) — unopposed catecholamine release can cause fatal hypertensive crisis. If confirmed, alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) must be established for 10–14 days before surgery; beta-blockade is added only AFTER alpha-blockade.
reference: Pacak K et al. Ann Intern Med 2001;134:315-329

question_text: A 62-year-old man has a horseshoe kidney found incidentally. Which of the following complications is MOST associated with horseshoe kidney?
option_a: Increased risk of clear cell renal cell carcinoma at 2x the general population rate
option_b: Ureteropelvic junction (UPJ) obstruction due to anomalous crossing vessels
option_c: Hypertension from bilateral renal artery stenosis
explanation: Horseshoe kidney is the most common renal fusion anomaly (1:400). The lower poles fuse anterior to the aorta and IVC, preventing normal ascent. The most common complication is UPJ obstruction (~30% of patients), caused by anomalous/aberrant vessels crossing the UPJ anteriorly and high insertion of the ureter into the renal pelvis, leading to hydronephrosis. Other associations include nephrolithiasis (~50%), pyelonephritis, increased risk of transitional cell carcinoma and Wilms tumor (not specifically clear cell RCC). UPJ obstruction may require dismembered pyeloplasty.
reference: Natsis K et al. Folia Morphol 2014;73:1-13

question_text: A 55-year-old man with prior aortic aneurysm repair presents with bilateral hydronephrosis and renal insufficiency. CT shows soft tissue density surrounding the periaortic region encasing the ureters with medial deviation of the ureters. No lymphadenopathy is seen. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Retroperitoneal fibrosis (RPF) is characterized by a fibro-inflammatory rind of tissue encasing the aorta and IVC, causing medial deviation and obstruction of the ureters. Medial ureteral deviation is the classic distinguishing feature — in contrast to lymphoma or sarcoma, which displace ureters laterally by bulky masses. RPF may be idiopathic (Ormond disease), secondary to drugs (methysergide, beta-blockers), malignancy (desmoplastic metastases), or post-surgical/post-radiation. Acute RPF enhances on CT. Steroids are first-line treatment for idiopathic RPF; ureteral stenting for obstruction.
reference: Caiafa RO et al. Radiographics 2013;33:1587-1600

question_text: A 75-year-old woman undergoes CT for hematuria. A sessile filling defect is identified in the bladder on CT urogram, measuring 3 cm with irregular margins and early arterial phase enhancement. What is the most appropriate next step?
option_a: Repeat CT in 3 months to assess stability
option_d: MRI of the bladder for staging
explanation: A sessile, enhancing bladder mass with irregular margins in an elderly patient with hematuria is highly suspicious for urothelial carcinoma. CT urogram can identify the lesion but cannot provide tissue diagnosis. Cystoscopy with TURBT (transurethral resection of bladder tumor) is the gold standard — it provides direct visualization, tissue histology, and assessment of muscle invasion depth. MRI is used for staging of known bladder cancer (T2 = muscle invasion) AFTER tissue diagnosis, not for initial diagnosis. CT is excellent for detecting lesions ≥5 mm but tissue is always required.
reference: Wolff EM et al. J Urol 2009;182:28-35

question_text: A 64-year-old man with elevated PSA undergoes multiparametric MRI. A 12mm lesion in the right peripheral zone shows marked T2 hypointensity, markedly restricted diffusion (ADC ~600 mm²/s) with markedly high signal on b=1400 images, and positive DCE (early asymmetric enhancement). According to PI-RADS v2.1, what score applies?
option_d: PI-RADS 5 — highly suspicious for clinically significant cancer
explanation: In PI-RADS v2.1, for the peripheral zone, DWI is the dominant sequence. Markedly restricted diffusion (markedly low ADC + markedly high signal on high b-value images) in a lesion <15 mm = DWI score 4. In the peripheral zone, a DWI score of 4 + positive DCE (early asymmetric arterial enhancement) upgrades the overall score to PI-RADS 5. A DWI score 4 WITHOUT positive DCE = PI-RADS 4. PI-RADS 5 carries very high probability (>80%) of clinically significant prostate cancer (Gleason grade group ≥2).
reference: Turkbey B et al. Eur Urol 2019;76:340-351

question_text: A 45-year-old woman presents with acute right flank pain. Non-contrast CT shows a 6 mm calcification at the right ureterovesical junction with moderate right hydroureteronephrosis. Stone density is 800 HU. What is the primary predictor of spontaneous stone passage, and what is the expected passage rate for this stone?
option_a: Stone density (800 HU) — high density predicts near-zero spontaneous passage
option_b: Stone size and location — 6 mm at UVJ, expected ~50–60% passage with medical expulsive therapy
option_c: Stone composition — calcium oxalate stones never pass spontaneously
option_d: Degree of hydronephrosis — moderate hydronephrosis requires immediate intervention
explanation: The primary predictors of spontaneous passage are stone SIZE and LOCATION. Stones ≤4 mm: >80% pass spontaneously. 5–7 mm stones at the UVJ: ~50–60% pass, especially with medical expulsive therapy (alpha-blockers such as tamsulosin, which relax the distal ureteral smooth muscle). >7 mm: <20% spontaneous passage, intervention often required. Stone density (HU) reflects composition and hardness but is secondary to size/location for predicting passage. Calcium oxalate/phosphate stones (most common, ~80%) are densest (typically >600 HU). Uric acid stones are radiolucent on plain film and low-density on CT (50–200 HU) — they respond to urine alkalinization.
reference: Türk C et al. Eur Urol 2022;82:1-47

question_text: A 38-year-old woman with known fibroids presents for MRI prior to uterine fibroid embolization (UFE). One fibroid measures 7 cm with complete T2 hypointensity. Another 4 cm fibroid shows markedly T2 hyperintense signal. What does this T2 signal difference predict about UFE response?
option_a: Both fibroids will respond equally well to UFE
option_b: The T2 hypointense fibroid will respond better; T2 hyperintense (cellular) fibroids have lower response rates
option_c: T2 hyperintense fibroids respond better due to higher vascularity
option_d: T2 signal has no predictive value for UFE response
explanation: T2 signal intensity of fibroids predicts UFE response. Classic (hypocellular, fibrous) fibroids show low T2 signal and have excellent UFE response rates (80–90% volume reduction). T2 hyperintense (cellular) leiomyomas have higher water content, more viable cells, and less fibrous stroma — they are more likely to have residual perfusion post-embolization and show lower volume reduction rates. Multiple studies including the FIBROID Registry confirm T2 signal as a significant predictor. Pre-UFE MRI maps fibroid number, location, size, and T2 signal characteristics for counseling and planning.
reference: deSouza NM et al. Radiology 2006;240:480-490

question_text: A 58-year-old man with a history of Lynch syndrome undergoes screening CT. A 9 cm left retroperitoneal mass is found with heterogeneous soft tissue density, areas of myxoid change, and no macroscopic fat. It does not arise from a visceral organ. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Leiomyosarcoma is the most common primary retroperitoneal sarcoma and the most common malignant retroperitoneal tumor in adults after lymphoma. It arises from smooth muscle of the IVC or retroperitoneal soft tissue. CT shows a large heterogeneous soft tissue mass with areas of necrosis and myxoid degeneration but without macroscopic fat (distinguishing it from liposarcoma). Well-differentiated liposarcoma (WD-LPS) is the most common retroperitoneal sarcoma overall and contains macroscopic fat with thick septa or non-adipose nodules. Lymphoma causes bulky lymphadenopathy, not a discrete non-nodal mass. Desmoid tumors are rare, infiltrative, and associated with FAP/APC mutation.
reference: Strauss DC et al. Br J Surg 2010;97:698-706

question_text: A large retroperitoneal mass encases the aorta and IVC, displacing the ureters laterally. CT shows multiple enlarged confluent nodes above and below the diaphragm. The patient has B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss). What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) is the most common retroperitoneal malignancy and can cause bulky confluent lymphadenopathy encasing the great vessels — the 'CT angiogram sign' (patent vessels surrounded by tumor). Key distinguishing feature: lymphoma DISPLACES the ureters LATERALLY (contrast with RPF, which causes MEDIAL deviation). B symptoms (fever, night sweats, >10% weight loss) and above/below-diaphragm involvement are hallmarks of advanced lymphoma (Stage III–IV). NHL is far more common than Hodgkin disease in the retroperitoneum. Treatment is systemic chemotherapy; biopsy is required for tissue diagnosis.
reference: Rajiah P et al. Radiographics 2008;28:625-639

question_text: A 70-year-old woman with a known history of breast cancer presents with an incidentally discovered 4 cm right adrenal mass. On non-contrast CT the mass measures 28 HU. APW is 55% and RPW is 37%. What is the most appropriate interpretation?
option_a: Benign adenoma — no further workup needed
option_b: Indeterminate — the washout values do not confirm adenoma, raising concern for metastasis in this clinical context
explanation: APW <60% and RPW <40% mean the mass does not meet CT washout criteria for adenoma. In a patient with known primary malignancy (breast cancer), an indeterminate adrenal mass raises significant concern for metastasis. Next steps: chemical shift MRI (if shows signal drop on opposed-phase, favors adenoma), or FDG-PET/CT (avid uptake favors metastasis). CT-guided biopsy can confirm metastasis if MRI/PET are indeterminate. Importantly, rule out pheochromocytoma before any biopsy (biochemical testing). For a patient with known malignancy, even a slightly indeterminate mass warrants workup — adenomas are common (prevalence 2–4%), but metastases must be excluded.
reference: Fassnacht M et al. Eur J Endocrinol 2016;175:G1-G34

question_text: A 35-year-old woman with MEN2A (RET proto-oncogene mutation) presents for screening imaging. MRI of the adrenals shows bilateral adrenal masses with markedly high T2 signal ('light bulb' sign) and avid contrast enhancement. 24-hour urine metanephrines are markedly elevated bilaterally. What is the diagnosis and surgical approach?
option_a: Bilateral adrenal adenomas — no surgery required
option_b: Bilateral pheochromocytomas — cortical-sparing adrenalectomy preferred to prevent Addisonian crisis
option_c: Bilateral adrenocortical carcinomas — bilateral adrenalectomy required
option_d: Bilateral adrenal metastases from medullary thyroid carcinoma
explanation: MEN2A (RET mutation) is associated with medullary thyroid carcinoma, hyperparathyroidism, and bilateral pheochromocytomas in approximately 50% of patients. The marked T2 hyperintensity ('light bulb' sign) and elevated metanephrines confirm bilateral pheochromocytoma. Cortical-sparing (partial) adrenalectomy is preferred for bilateral disease to preserve adrenocortical function and avoid lifelong steroid replacement (Addison disease). Alpha-blockade must be established before surgery. If cortical-sparing is not feasible, bilateral adrenalectomy necessitates lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement.
reference: Lenders JW et al. Lancet 2005;366:665-675

question_text: A 65-year-old man with known lung cancer presents with a 7 cm left adrenal mass. CT shows a heterogeneous mass with irregular margins, central necrosis, and involvement of the left renal vein. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: In a patient with known primary malignancy (lung cancer), an adrenal mass is far more likely to represent a metastasis than a primary adrenal tumor. Lung, breast, melanoma, colon, and renal cell carcinoma are the most common primary tumors to metastasize to the adrenal gland. CT features of adrenal metastasis: heterogeneous, does not meet adenoma washout criteria, may be bilateral. ACC is a primary adrenal malignancy (rare, incidence 1–2/million/year), typically >6 cm, with Cushing syndrome or virilization, vascular invasion. The combination of known primary malignancy + indeterminate adrenal mass strongly favors metastasis. FDG-PET or biopsy (after biochemical pheo exclusion) confirms diagnosis.
reference: Fassnacht M et al. Eur J Endocrinol 2016;175:G1-G34

question_text: A 22-year-old man presents with acute right scrotal pain and swelling. Scrotal ultrasound shows a heterogeneously enlarged epididymis with marked hyperemia on color Doppler, and increased blood flow in the adjacent testis. Pyuria is present on urinalysis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Epididymo-orchitis presents with scrotal pain and swelling, typically with fever and urinary symptoms. Ultrasound shows an enlarged, hypoechoic epididymis with markedly increased Doppler flow — the opposite of testicular torsion, which shows absent flow. Concurrent orchitis (involvement of testis) is present in ~30% of cases. Most common organisms in young sexually active men: Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae. In older men: coliform bacteria (E. coli). Pyuria supports the diagnosis. Testicular torsion must be considered first in any acute scrotum — absent Doppler flow or whirlpool sign mandates immediate surgical exploration.
reference: Bhatt S et al. Radiographics 2011;31:1959-1976

question_text: A 45-year-old male with recurrent nephrolithiasis and no prior surgery undergoes Tc-99m MAG3 diuretic renography. The right kidney shows delayed tracer excretion and a diuretic washout half-time (T1/2) of 35 minutes. The left kidney is normal. What is the diagnosis?
option_a: Renal artery stenosis causing ischemic nephropathy
explanation: Diuretic renography (MAG3 or DTPA) is the functional study of choice for suspected UPJ obstruction. After furosemide injection, a non-obstructed system drains rapidly (T1/2 <10 minutes). An obstructed system shows persistent tracer accumulation with T1/2 >20 minutes. Values of 10–20 minutes are equivocal. This patient's T1/2 of 35 minutes confirms obstruction. UPJ obstruction is the most common cause of hydronephrosis in children and young adults and may present with recurrent stones or pain. CT can show the anatomic narrowing; MAG3 confirms functional significance and provides differential renal function.
reference: Shulkin BL et al. J Urol 1997;157:2041-2046

question_text: A 35-year-old man with a history of pelvic trauma 10 years ago and now presenting with weak urinary stream undergoes retrograde urethrogram (RUG). A short segment (1 cm) filling defect is seen in the bulbar urethra. The mucosa proximal to the defect appears normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
option_a: Transitional cell carcinoma of the urethra
option_b: Urethral stricture of the bulbar urethra
explanation: Urethral stricture is a fibrotic narrowing of the urethral lumen, most commonly affecting the bulbar urethra (the most common site of stricture overall). Causes: trauma (pelvic fracture, straddle injury), prior instrumentation/catheterization, infection (gonococcal urethritis), or idiopathic. RUG is the first-line imaging study — it shows a focal filling defect with proximal dilation and may quantify length, which guides treatment (dilation vs. urethroplasty). Short strictures (<1–2 cm) may be amenable to dilation or direct visual internal urethrotomy (DVIU); longer strictures require formal urethroplasty. Posterior urethral valves occur in male neonates, not adults.
reference: Mundy AR et al. Eur Urol 2011;60:468-474

question_text: A 60-year-old man with known von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease undergoes surveillance CT. Multiple bilateral small enhancing renal masses are seen alongside multiple simple cysts. The largest solid mass measures 2.8 cm. What is the most appropriate management?
option_b: Surveillance — intervene when the largest solid lesion reaches 3 cm
option_c: Systemic therapy with a VEGF inhibitor
option_d: Bilateral renal biopsy to confirm RCC histology before any intervention
explanation: VHL disease is an autosomal dominant condition characterized by clear cell RCCs, renal cysts, hemangioblastomas (cerebellum/spine/retina), pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, and pheochromocytomas. Multiple bilateral clear cell RCCs are essentially universal. The 3 cm threshold for intervention is the standard in VHL management — lesions <3 cm are observed (very low metastatic potential), while those reaching 3 cm undergo nephron-sparing surgery (partial nephrectomy or ablation) to preserve renal function across multiple procedures over a lifetime. Radical nephrectomy is avoided to prevent dialysis dependency. All solid renal masses in VHL are treated as clear cell RCC without biopsy.
reference: Maher ER et al. J Med Genet 2011;48:439-444

question_text: A 3-day-old neonate has an incidentally detected left adrenal mass on abdominal ultrasound. It is echogenic and measures 3 cm. Repeat ultrasound at 2 weeks shows the mass is now predominantly cystic with peripheral calcification. Urine catecholamines are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Neonatal adrenal hemorrhage is the most common cause of an adrenal mass in the neonate. It occurs due to birth trauma, hypoxia, or coagulopathy. The classic imaging evolution is diagnostic: initially echogenic (acute blood) → then heterogeneous/complex → cystic with peripheral calcium over days to weeks. Normal catecholamines distinguish it from neuroblastoma, which may also present as an adrenal mass in neonates but shows stable or growing mass, elevated catecholamines, and does not involute. Serial ultrasound is the preferred follow-up modality — the hemorrhage should involute over weeks to months. MRI can confirm hemorrhage if needed.
reference: Mutlu M et al. Pediatr Neonatol 2011;52:285-292

question_text: A 32-year-old woman presents with acute pelvic pain. Pelvic ultrasound shows an enlarged right ovary measuring 6 cm with multiple peripheral follicles and absent blood flow on Doppler. What is the most likely diagnosis and most important next step?
option_a: Ovarian cyst — recommend OCP and repeat US in 6 weeks
option_c: Dermoid cyst — elective surgery in 2–4 weeks
option_d: Tubo-ovarian abscess — IV antibiotics and gynecology referral
explanation: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. Classic US findings: enlarged ovary (>4 cm, usually with a mass/cyst as lead point), peripheral follicles displaced to the periphery by edema, and absent or reversed venous Doppler flow (arterial flow may be preserved in partial torsion). The whirlpool sign of the ovarian pedicle may be seen. Importantly, the presence of Doppler flow does NOT exclude torsion. Delay in surgery leads to irreversible ischemia. Surgical detorsion (with or without ovarian cystectomy) is performed; oophorectomy only if the ovary is non-viable. Teratomas/dermoids are the most common lead-point mass in reproductive age women.
reference: Bhatt S et al. Radiographics 2008;28:1355-1368

question_text: A 55-year-old woman with postmenopausal bleeding undergoes pelvic MRI. The endometrial mass shows disruption of the junctional zone with myometrial invasion extending >50% of the myometrial thickness. The cervix is not involved. No lymphadenopathy or extrauterine spread is seen. What is the FIGO stage?
explanation: FIGO 2023 staging for endometrial carcinoma: Stage IA = myometrial invasion <50% (or confined to endometrium). Stage IB = myometrial invasion ≥50%, no cervical stromal invasion, no extrauterine spread. Stage II = cervical stromal invasion. Stage IIIA = serosal or adnexal involvement. Stage IIIB = vaginal or parametrial involvement. Stage IIIC = lymph node metastases. Stage IVA = bladder or bowel mucosal invasion. This case shows ≥50% myometrial invasion without cervical involvement = FIGO IB. MRI is the preferred modality for local staging — the low T2 JZ/myometrial boundary is assessed for depth of invasion.
reference: Colombo N et al. Ann Oncol 2023;34:897-913

question_text: A 28-year-old woman with primary amenorrhea undergoes pelvic MRI. There is a single uterine horn pointing to the right, a rudimentary non-communicating left horn, and a single right kidney. The vagina is present. What is the diagnosis?
option_b: Unicornuate uterus with rudimentary horn (Müllerian class II)
explanation: Unicornuate uterus results from failure of one Müllerian duct to develop fully. The classic imaging appearance is a banana-shaped, laterally deviated uterine horn. A rudimentary (non-communicating) contralateral horn may be present and can cause cyclic pain due to trapped menstrual blood (hematometra). Importantly, unicornuate uterus has the highest obstetric complication rate of all Müllerian anomalies. Associated ipsilateral renal agenesis occurs in 40% of cases — the absent left kidney here is characteristic. Bicornuate uterus shows two horns with a fundal cleft >1 cm on MRI. Didelphys has two separate uterine bodies and cervices.
reference: Grimbizis GF et al. Hum Reprod 2013;28:2032-2044

question_text: On pelvic ultrasound, a 3 cm adnexal lesion is seen in a 58-year-old postmenopausal woman. It is unilocular, anechoic, with a thin regular wall, no solid components, no septa, and no Doppler flow. What is the O-RADS US score and recommended management?
option_b: O-RADS 2 — almost certainly benign, 1-year follow-up ultrasound
option_c: O-RADS 3 — low risk, 8–12 week follow-up ultrasound
explanation: The ACR O-RADS US lexicon (2019) stratifies adnexal lesions by malignancy risk. O-RADS 2 = almost certainly benign (malignancy risk <1%): includes simple unilocular cysts of any size in any patient age, with no solid components, thin regular walls, and no flow. Management: 1-year follow-up US recommended for postmenopausal women with simple cysts >1 cm (previously per-ACR guidelines); some guidelines allow no follow-up for cysts <5 cm in postmenopausal women given extremely low malignancy risk. O-RADS 3 (low risk, 1–10% malignancy) would apply to unilocular cysts with low-level echoes. The key O-RADS 2 features: no solid component, thin regular walls, no Doppler flow.
reference: Andreotti RF et al. Radiology 2020;294:168-185

question_text: A 50-year-old man with hypertension undergoes renal MRI. The right kidney shows a 3.5 cm mass in the upper pole. The mass is T2 hypointense relative to renal cortex, shows homogeneous enhancement, and has a central stellate scar on delayed images. No lymphadenopathy is seen. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Renal oncocytoma is a benign renal neoplasm arising from intercalated cells of the collecting duct. Classic imaging features: well-defined, homogeneous, T2 hypointense, homogeneous enhancement, and a central stellate scar on delayed imaging. The spoke-wheel pattern of enhancement is a classic but insensitive finding. Crucially, no imaging feature can reliably distinguish oncocytoma from chromophobe RCC or clear cell RCC — surgical resection or ablation is required for definitive diagnosis. Clear cell RCC shows marked heterogeneous enhancement and is typically T2 hyperintense. Chromophobe RCC may also be T2 hypointense with a scar but lacks the homogeneous enhancement pattern.
reference: Chawla SN et al. J Urol 2010;183:1485-1489

question_text: A 35-year-old man presents 3 days after a motor vehicle accident with frank hematuria. CT with contrast shows a 6 cm right renal laceration extending through the collecting system with active contrast extravasation into the perinephric space and perirenal hematoma. The renal vascular pedicle is intact. What is the AAST renal trauma grade?
option_b: Grade III — laceration >1 cm without collecting system involvement
option_c: Grade IV — laceration through collecting system with urinary extravasation
option_d: Grade V — shattered kidney or vascular pedicle injury
explanation: The AAST renal injury scale (2018 revision): Grade I = contusion or non-expanding subcapsular hematoma. Grade II = perinephric hematoma confined to retroperitoneum, <1 cm laceration not involving collecting system. Grade III = laceration >1 cm without collecting system involvement. Grade IV = laceration through the collecting system with urinary extravasation OR segmental renal artery/vein injury with contained hematoma. Grade V = shattered kidney OR main renal artery/vein avulsion. This case shows a laceration extending through the collecting system with extravasation = Grade IV. Management: Grade I–III often managed conservatively; Grade IV–V may require angioembolization or surgery.
reference: Kozar RA et al. J Trauma Acute Care Surg 2018;85:1119-1122

question_text: A 55-year-old man underwent renal transplantation 3 months ago. Creatinine is acutely rising. Renal transplant ultrasound shows increased cortical echogenicity, mildly elevated resistive indices (RI 0.80–0.85), and absence of diastolic flow reversal. Biopsy confirms T-cell mediated acute rejection. What imaging finding would suggest the most severe form of vascular rejection?
option_a: Mildly elevated RI (0.75–0.80) with preserved diastolic flow
option_b: Reversal of diastolic flow (RI >1.0) indicating critical vascular compromise
explanation: Renal transplant Doppler assessment uses the resistive index (RI = [peak systolic velocity − end-diastolic velocity] / peak systolic velocity). Normal transplant RI: 0.50–0.70. Elevated RI (>0.70) is nonspecific and seen in rejection, ATN, obstruction, and pyelonephritis. Reversal of diastolic flow (negative end-diastolic velocity, RI >1.0) represents critical compromise of the diastolic circulation and is associated with severe acute rejection, acute tubular necrosis, or renal vein thrombosis. Prompt intervention is required. Renal vein thrombosis is a surgical emergency and classically shows reversed diastolic flow with absent venous signal.
reference: Kolonko A et al. Transplant Proc 2009;41:3015-3017

question_text: A 58-year-old woman with an elevated PSA (wait — a 58-year-old man) undergoes mpMRI. A 15 mm lesion is identified in the right transition zone showing homogeneous T2 hypointensity with well-defined margins, a lenticular (lens-shaped) configuration, and no restricted diffusion. What is the PI-RADS v2.1 score?
explanation: In the transition zone, T2 is the dominant sequence for PI-RADS v2.1 scoring. A well-defined, homogeneous, lenticular (lens-shaped) TZ lesion with T2 hypointensity = PI-RADS TZ score 2, consistent with a BPH nodule. Key T2 features distinguishing BPH from TZ cancer: BPH nodules are homogeneous, lenticular, and well-defined; TZ cancers are heterogeneous with ill-defined margins, have a 'erased charcoal' appearance, and may show restricted diffusion. The absence of restricted diffusion further supports a benign BPH nodule. PI-RADS 4 TZ = circumscribed homogeneous non-encapsulated hypointense mass (≥15 mm) with no feature to call benign. PI-RADS 3 = heterogeneous but not fitting score 4 or 5.
reference: PI-RADS Steering Committee. Radiology 2019;292:278-296

question_text: A 70-year-old man with Gleason 4+4 prostate cancer undergoes staging MRI. A 22 mm lesion abuts the right neurovascular bundle over a contact length of 12 mm with obliteration of the rectoprostatic angle and a bulge in the capsular contour. What is the finding and its staging significance?
option_a: Confined disease — T2a (tumor in one lobe, <50% of lobe)
explanation: Extracapsular extension (ECE) represents tumor penetrating beyond the prostatic capsule = pT3a. MRI findings associated with ECE: capsular abutment >10 mm contact length (most specific), obliteration of the rectoprostatic angle, capsular irregularity or spiculation, asymmetric thickening of the neurovascular bundle, and measurable extracapsular tumor. Accurate ECE staging impacts surgical planning — nerve-sparing surgery is not performed on the affected side. MRI has moderate sensitivity (~60%) but high specificity (~90%) for ECE detection. Seminal vesicle invasion (T3b) shows tumor extension into the seminal vesicle with loss of T2 signal.
reference: Cornud F et al. Eur Radiol 2002;12:121-135

question_text: A 55-year-old man with hematuria has CT urogram showing a midline anterior bladder mass at the dome with calcification and an extraperitoneal component extending into the space of Retzius. Cystoscopy confirms a mucin-secreting adenocarcinoma. What is the most likely primary tumor origin?
option_a: Urothelial carcinoma of the bladder dome
explanation: Urachal carcinoma is a rare but distinctive bladder tumor arising from remnants of the urachus (the fibromuscular cord connecting the bladder dome to the umbilicus). Characteristic features: midline/supravesical location at the bladder DOME, calcification within the lesion (~70%), extraperitoneal extension into the prevesical space (space of Retzius), and mucin-secreting adenocarcinoma histology. The Sheldon classification guides staging. Treatment is en bloc resection including the umbilicus and urachal ligament. Prognosis is poor due to late presentation. Standard urothelial TCC of the dome can occur but lacks the calcification, mucin secretion, and characteristic midline extraperitoneal extension.
reference: Pinthus JH et al. Eur Urol 2006;50:1399-1406
